channel.title: Reverse-Engineering on SPERIXLABS
channel.description: Recent content in Reverse-Engineering on SPERIXLABS

item.title: How I Decrypted Obfuscated Mobile API Payloads
item.description:
I’ll tell this exactly how it happened.

I started with an Android app whose network endpoints returned text files that looked like gibberish. At first glance, they looked Base64-ish, but decoding them still produced high-entropy binary. No JSON. No obvious compression headers. No quick win.

Then static analysis hit a familiar wall.

Step 1: Static analysis looked promising… until it didn’t

I decompiled the APK and quickly found signs of a shell/packer layer (StubApp, native loader libs). That usually means the interesting logic is not where JADX wants it to be.

item.title: Unlocking the Vault: A Deep Dive into macOS ARM64 Trial Enforcement and Binary Patching
item.description:
Introduction

In the course of analyzing a decompiled macOS productivity application—a calendar and meeting alert utility with a time-limited trial—we encountered a multi-layered enforcement mechanism governing trial expiry, feature gating, and application termination. This practice is common in commercial software: when the trial expires, the app displays warnings, disables premium features, and eventually terminates unless the user purchases or restores a license.

This article documents the complete reverse engineering journey: from initial string reconnaissance through call-graph tracing, assembly-level analysis, and ultimately the development of six binary patches that bypass trial termination, re-enable gated features, and customize the UI—all without modifying the application’s source code or runtime environment.
